title: The Complete History of Tamriel
subtitle: A Comprehensive Chronicle from the Dawn Era to the Fourth Era
quote: Before the Ages of Man, the et'Ada — the original spirits — shaped the world. What followed was history, myth, war, empire, and legend intertwined across thousands of years.

eras.name: The Dawn Era
eras.subtitle: Before Time, Before History
eras.overview: The mythological age before mortal history, in which the gods created the world and the fundamental forces of the universe were established.

sections.title: The Creation of Mundus
sections.content: In the beginning, there was no world. There was only Aetherius — the realm of pure magic and immortality — and Oblivion — the infinite void, home of chaos and the Daedric Princes. The great cosmic forces of creation were embodied by two supreme spirits: Anu (the force of stasis, order, and light) and Padomay (the force of change, chaos, and darkness). Their interplay gave rise to all things. From Anu came the soul Anui-El, and from Anui-El came Auri-El — the spirit who became the Elvish god of time and the sun. From Padomay came Sithis — the void and the embodiment of entropy. Between these polarities arose the et'Ada — the original spirits — among the most powerful of which were the Aedra (who would sacrifice themselves to create the world) and the Daedra (who refused sacrifice and retreated to Oblivion).

sections.title: Lorkhan and the Mortal Plane
sections.content: Among the et'Ada was Lorkhan — called Shor by Nords, Lorkhaj by Khajiit, Sep by Redguards — a trickster-spirit of ambition and wandering. Lorkhan conceived a plan to create a mortal plane, a world between Aetherius and Oblivion where the et'Ada could experience limitation, growth, and meaning. Each spirit who contributed to the creation of Mundus sacrificed a portion of their divine nature, becoming planets, moons, and stars. When spirits realized the full cost of sacrifice, they were furious. Auri-El led the charge against Lorkhan. The et'Ada tore out Lorkhan's divine heart and cast it to the mortal world. His body became the two moons of Nirn: Masser and Secunda. Lorkhan's Heart fell to Vvardenfell, embedding itself beneath Red Mountain — it would play a central role in history thousands of years later.

sections.title: The Earthbones and the Shaping of Nirn
sections.content: Many spirits who chose to help shape the world became the Earthbones — the fundamental laws of nature, physics, and life. They became so embedded in the world that they ceased to be truly divine and became the very rules by which Nirn operated: gravity, seasons, the cycle of life and death. The planet Nirn was formed with its massive continent Tamriel at the center, surrounded by seas. The continents of Akavir (east), Atmora (north), Yokuda (west), and Pyandonea (south) also existed.

sections.title: The Daedric Princes
sections.content: Those et'Ada who refused to participate in the creation of Mundus remained in Oblivion, each carving out their own infinite pocket realm. These became the Daedric Princes — immensely powerful beings who could not truly die but who regarded mortals primarily as amusement or tools.

eras.name: The Merethic Era
eras.subtitle: The Age of Elves
eras.overview: The era of Elven dominance over Tamriel, before the arrival of Men in significant numbers. Ancient races established their civilizations, and the foundations of all later conflict were laid.

sections.title: The Aldmer: The First People
sections.content: The first mortal beings to walk Tamriel were the Aldmer — the First Folk, or Elves. They came from the mythical island of Aldmeris and spread across Tamriel over thousands of years. They were tall, long-lived, highly intelligent beings with an innate connection to magic. Over millennia, the Aldmer diverged into distinct elven races shaped by their environments and beliefs.

elven_races.name: Chimer
elven_races.note: Followed the prophet Veloth, worshipped Daedra

elven_races.name: Dwemer
elven_races.note: Rejected religion in favor of science and reason

elven_races.name: Falmer
elven_races.note: Settled the frozen north, later devolved under the Dwemer

elven_races.name: Bosmer
elven_races.note: Adopted the Green Pact with nature

elven_races.name: Altmer
elven_races.note: Remained most genetically and culturally pure

elven_races.name: Orsimer
elven_races.note: Transformed and cursed when their god Trinimac was devoured by Boethiah

elven_races.name: Ayleids
elven_races.note: Built the White-Gold Tower and enslaved early Men

sections.title: The Ayleids and the White-Gold Tower
sections.content: In Cyrodiil, the Ayleids built a magnificent civilization. Their greatest achievement was the White-Gold Tower — a massive metaphysical structure designed to emulate the Adamantine Tower, the first structure ever built on Nirn. The Ayleids practiced complex Daedric and Aedric magical traditions and enslaved the primitive Nede (proto-humans, ancestors of the Imperials) who had migrated to Cyrodiil from Atmora.

sections.title: The Dwemer: Masters of the Deep
sections.content: The Dwemer built extraordinary underground cities filled with brass automata, steam-powered machinery, and Aetherium constructs. They were perhaps the greatest inventors and engineers Tamriel ever produced. Uniquely atheistic, they rejected the divinity of the Aedra and Daedra, studying instead the nature of tonal architecture — the idea that reality's fundamental laws are expressed as sound and vibration, and that one with sufficient knowledge could alter those laws. Persistent conflict with the Chimer (who had settled the same region) defined much of their existence.

sections.title: The Falmer: The Snow Elves' Tragedy
sections.content: The Falmer — Snow Elves — built an advanced civilization in Skyrim, including the great cathedral Auri-El's Chantry. Their downfall began when the Atmorans (ancestors of the Nords) arrived from Atmora. After the Night of Tears — a catastrophic Snow Elf attack on the human settlement of Saarthal — the great Atmoran leader Ysgramor returned with his Five Hundred Companions and systematically drove the Snow Elves from Skyrim. The few survivors fled to the Dwemer, who agreed to shelter them but forced them to eat a toxic fungus that blinded and devolved them over generations into the bestial Falmer known in later eras. One Snow Elf — Knight-Paladin Gelebor — refused this fate, remaining in Auri-El's Chantry in near-stasis for thousands of years.

sections.title: The Dragon War
sections.content: The Dragon War was the rebellion of humanity against dragonkind. The dragon Alduin and his kin ruled over early Nord civilization, demanding worship and tribute. Human Dragon Priests served as intermediaries, wielding terrifying power. The dragon Paarthurnax — Alduin's lieutenant and brother — defected to humanity's side and taught the ancient Nords the Way of the Voice (the Thu'um). The heroes Hakon One-Eye, Gormlaith Golden-Hilt, and Felldir the Old led the revolt. Unable to kill Alduin outright, they used an Elder Scroll to cast him forward through time. Paarthurnax founded High Hrothgar and the Greybeards afterward.

eras.name: The First Era
eras.subtitle: Rise of Men and Empires
eras.overview: The age in which humanity rose to challenge Elvish dominance. Great empires formed and fell, the Dwemer vanished, and the foundations of the modern world were laid.

sections.title: The Nordic Expansion
sections.content: The First Era began when the Nordic king Harald unified the scattered Atmoran-descended clans of Skyrim and launched expansionist campaigns. The Nords spread their dominion across much of Tamriel, pushing against the Elven civilizations. Nordic dragon cults flourished during this period as ancient dragons dominated human societies through the power of the Thu'um.

sections.title: The Alessian Revolt
sections.content: In Cyrodiil, the human slaves known as the Nedes had suffered under Ayleid rule for generations. The revolution was sparked by Alessia, a slave-woman who claimed divine visions and the blessing of Akatosh. Allied with Nordic armies, she led a successful revolt against the Ayleid city-states. Alessia became the first Empress of the Alessian Empire, ruling from the White-Gold Tower. She created a syncretic religion combining Aedric and Nordic pantheons — the forerunner of the Eight Divines. The Amulet of Kings — a covenant between Alessia and Akatosh — was forged, creating a barrier between Mundus and Oblivion that would persist as long as a Dragonborn emperor sat on the throne.
sections.significance: First human empire in Cyrodiil; foundation of the Divine Covenant protecting Mundus from Oblivion

sections.title: The Alessian Order and Theocratic Tyranny
sections.content: After Alessia's death, her empire was eventually dominated by the fanatical Alessian Order, founded by the monk Marukh. The Order preached extreme monotheism and persecuted both Elven and syncretic religious traditions. Its Exclusionary Mandates barred Elves from trading in Imperial markets. The Order grew increasingly totalitarian, eventually controlling the empire itself. It was ultimately broken at the Battle of Glenumbria Moors (1E 482), where a coalition of Direnni Elves and Breton and Nordic forces crushed its armies.

sections.title: The Battle of Red Mountain
sections.content: The greatest cataclysm of the First Era came from Morrowind. The Dwemer tonemaster Kagrenac discovered that the Heart of Lorkhan still pulsed with divine power beneath Red Mountain. He devised three sacred tools — Sunder, Keening, and Wraithguard — to tap the Heart's power and transform his people into living gods. The Chimer leader Nerevar Indoril discovered this and confronted Kagrenac. The Battle of Red Mountain erupted between Chimer and Dwemer. When Kagrenac struck the Heart with his tools, all the Dwemer vanished simultaneously everywhere in the world — their fate remains Tamriel's greatest mystery. Nerevar was mortally wounded. The Tribunal — Vivec, Almalexia, and Sotha Sil — then used Kagrenac's Tools on the Heart themselves, ascending to godhood against Nerevar's wishes. Azura's fury was total: she cursed the entire Chimer race, turning their golden skin grey and their eyes red. They became the Dunmer. Azura also prophesied the Nerevarine — Nerevar's reincarnation — who would one day challenge the Tribunal's power.
sections.significance: The Dwemer vanished; the Tribunal became gods; the Chimer became Dunmer; set the stage for events 2,700 years later in the Third Era

sections.title: The Reman Dynasty and the Second Empire
sections.content: The Akaviri Invasion of 1E 2703 reshaped the continent. Warriors from Akavir invaded through Skyrim. At Pale Pass in the Jerall Mountains, they were confronted by Reman Cyrodiil, a mysterious Dragonborn man. Recognizing him as Dragonborn, the Akaviri armies immediately surrendered and knelt before him. Reman forged the Second Empire — a genuinely expansionist military power that brought Cyrodiil, Skyrim, High Rock, Hammerfell, and parts of Morrowind under Imperial rule. The Akaviri Potentates became crucial advisors. The dynasty ended when Emperor Reman III and his heir were assassinated by the Morag Tong, after which the Akaviri Potentate Versidue-Shaie assumed power.
sections.significance: Established the Second Empire and Akaviri-Imperial cultural fusion that would define Tamriel's military tradition

eras.name: The Second Era
eras.subtitle: The Interregnum and the Forgotten Crown
eras.overview: An era of chaos following the collapse of the Reman Empire. The Interregnum saw nearly 400 years of war and fragmentation, culminating in Tiber Septim's unification of Tamriel.

sections.title: The Akaviri Potentate and the Rise of the Guilds
sections.content: The Akaviri Potentates Versidue-Shaie and later Savirien-Chorak ruled competently but were increasingly resented. One of their most lasting contributions was the Guilds Act (2E 321), which officially authorized the creation of multi-province guilds with legal standing across the Empire — giving rise to the Fighters Guild and Mages Guild as continent-spanning institutions. Versidue-Shaie was assassinated by the Morag Tong in 2E 324. In 2E 430, the last Akaviri Potentate and all his heirs were killed in a single night. The Second Empire collapsed. Tamriel entered the Interregnum — nearly 400 years of chaos.

sections.title: The Interregnum — An Age of Chaos
sections.content: With no central authority, Tamriel fractured into dozens of warring kingdoms and alliances. This period saw plague, famine, and constant war across most of the continent. The rise and fall of countless petty kings and warlords defined the era. The Reachmen in the Reach (western Skyrim/High Rock borderlands) became a persistent destabilizing force. The arch-necromancer Mannimarco — the King of Worms — grew in power during this chaotic period, gathering undead armies. The three great alliances of the era slowly took shape.

sections.title: The Planemeld and Molag Bal's Invasion
sections.content: The central crisis of the Second Era was the Planemeld — the Daedric Prince Molag Bal's attempt to merge Nirn with his Oblivion plane, Coldharbour. His plan involved planting massive Dark Anchor structures across Tamriel to drag Nirn into Coldharbour. The arch-necromancer Mannimarco had allied with the would-be Emperor Varen Aquilarios in a scheme to relight the Dragonfires of the White-Gold Tower. However, Mannimarco had secretly made a deal with Molag Bal. He corrupted the ritual, shattering the covenant with Akatosh entirely — an event called the Soulburst — which broke the barrier between Mundus and Oblivion and enabled the Planemeld to begin. The Vestige (the player character of ESO), who had their soul stolen by Molag Bal, fought through the three-faction Alliance War while working to stop the Planemeld. The Daedric invasion was ultimately contained, but the three alliances continued fighting each other.

sections.title: Tiber Septim and the Founding of the Third Empire
sections.content: The chaos of the Interregnum was ended by Talos, known in history as Tiber Septim — born as Hjalti Early-Beard. He was a Dragonborn military commander who united the warring Nord kingdoms, defeated the Camoran Usurper of Valenwood, conquered High Rock and Hammerfell, and received the great Numidium — the brass god, a colossal automaton originally built by the Dwemer — from the battlemage Zurin Arctus and the Dunmer master Vivec (who surrendered his power willingly). Using Numidium, Tiber Septim crushed the Aldmeri Dominion and conquered Summerset and Valenwood. Morrowind surrendered diplomatically via the Armistice rather than being conquered outright. In 2E 896, Tiber Septim was crowned Emperor of All Tamriel. The Third Era began. After his death, Tiber Septim was apotheosized as the god Talos — the ninth divine, patron of mankind, war, and governance.
sections.significance: Unified all of Tamriel under one empire for the first time; established the Third Era and the Septim Dynasty

eras.name: The Third Era
eras.subtitle: The Septim Dynasty
eras.overview: The age of the Septim Empire — Tamriel's longest period of relative peace and unity. It ended in catastrophe when the Septim bloodline was extinguished and Mehrunes Dagon invaded the mortal world.

sections.title: The Height of the Septim Empire
sections.content: Under the Septim dynasty, Tamriel experienced its longest period of relative peace and prosperity. The Eight Divines (plus Talos) religion unified much of the continent. The Imperial City became the cultural and political heart of the world. Key emperors included Tiber Septim himself, followed by a series of successors — some capable, some troubled. Notably, Pelagius Septim III (known as Pelagius the Mad) became legendary for his insanity before dying young.

sections.title: The Eternal Champion and the Staff of Chaos
sections.content: Emperor Uriel Septim VII was imprisoned in Oblivion by the treacherous Imperial Battlemage Jagar Tharn, who assumed the Emperor's identity for years. The Eternal Champion assembled the shattered Staff of Chaos from pieces hidden across Tamriel, freed the Emperor, and defeated Jagar Tharn in the Imperial Palace.

sections.title: The Warp in the West (Miracle of Peace)
sections.content: The reconstructed Numidium was contested among six factions in High Rock. Through a Dragon Break — a fracture in linear time — all six factions received the Totem of Tiber Septim simultaneously. Numidium activated and rampaged for days, causing massive destruction. When the Dragon Break resolved, the fractured kingdoms of High Rock had merged into four larger, more stable kingdoms: Daggerfall, Wayrest, Sentinel, and Orsinium.

sections.title: Morrowind and the Tribunal's Twilight
sections.content: By the late Third Era, the power of the Tribunal — the living gods Vivec, Almalexia, and Sotha Sil — was fading. Lorkhan's Heart (the source of their divine power) was weakening. Almalexia had grown paranoid and violent. Sotha Sil had withdrawn entirely into his Clockwork City. Vivec barely held the Floating Ministry — a meteor he had suspended over Vivec City for centuries — in place. A prisoner arrived in Morrowind identified by Azura as the Nerevarine, the reincarnation of Nerevar. The Nerevarine discovered the truth of the Tribunal's origins, acquired Kagrenac's Tools, and ultimately destroyed the Heart of Lorkhan, severing the Tribunal's connection to divinity permanently. Dagoth Ur — an ancient Chimer lord who had also tapped into the Heart's power — was defeated as his citadel collapsed. Vivec faded. Almalexia was slain by the Nerevarine in her madness. Sotha Sil had already been murdered by Almalexia in his Clockwork City. The Tribunal's age was over.

sections.title: The Oblivion Crisis
sections.content: The defining catastrophe of the Third Era. Emperor Uriel Septim VII and all his known heirs were assassinated by the Mythic Dawn — a cult devoted to Mehrunes Dagon. The cult's leader, Mankar Camoran, had obtained the Mysterium Xarxes — a profane text written by Dagon himself. Without a Dragonborn Emperor to maintain the Dragonfires of the White-Gold Tower, the barrier between Mundus and Oblivion collapsed. Oblivion Gates opened across Tamriel, pouring Daedric armies into every province. The city of Kvatch was among the first to fall. The Hero of Kvatch discovered the last heir: Martin Septim, an illegitimate son of Uriel living as a priest. In the final confrontation, Martin Septim shattered the Amulet of Kings and called upon Akatosh directly. Akatosh manifested as an enormous Dragon God avatar and destroyed Mehrunes Dagon's physical manifestation, banishing him from Mundus. The cost was total: Martin burned away entirely and became a stone statue in the Temple District. The Septim bloodline was extinguished. The Dragonfires went dark permanently. The Third Era ended.
sections.significance: End of the Septim bloodline; permanent extinguishing of the Dragonfires; end of the Third Era

eras.name: The Fourth Era
eras.subtitle: A World Without the Septims
eras.overview: The age following the Oblivion Crisis. The Empire fragmented, new powers rose, and ultimately the World Eater Alduin returned — only to be defeated by a new Dragonborn.

sections.title: The Post-Septim Chaos
sections.content: With no Emperor and no Dragonborn heir, the Empire fractured. High Chancellor Ocato served as Potentate, attempting to hold the Empire together. Morrowind was struck by catastrophe — the Red Year (4E 5), when the volcano Red Mountain erupted catastrophically following the death of Vivec, who had held it in check. Vast swathes of Morrowind were destroyed. Hundreds of thousands of Dunmer refugees fled to Skyrim and Black Marsh. The Argonians — historically enslaved by the Dunmer — used the chaos to invade southern Morrowind, sacking Mournhold in a brutal reversal.

key_events.name: Argonian Invasion
key_events.description: Argonians invade southern Morrowind, sack Mournhold

sections.title: The Umbriel Crisis
sections.content: A mysterious floating city called Umbriel drifted across Tamriel, raising the dead into an undead army as it traveled. It devastated portions of Black Marsh, Morrowind, and Cyrodiil before ultimately being stopped.
key_events: Umbriel crosses Tamriel raising dead armies

sections.title: The Great War
sections.content: The Aldmeri Dominion had re-formed, now dominated by a radical faction of Altmer called the Thalmor, who believed Talos's apotheosis was a lie and that Men were inferior to Mer. In 4E 171, the Dominion sent an ultimatum to Emperor Titus Mede II: ban Talos worship, dissolve the Blades, and cede large territories. Titus Mede II refused. Dominion armies invaded from south and west, achieving shocking early successes and briefly capturing the Imperial City. Titus Mede II launched a desperate counterattack — the Battle of the Red Ring — which retook the Imperial City at enormous cost. After four years, both sides were exhausted. The White-Gold Concordat (4E 175) ended the war with terms deeply humiliating to the Empire: Talos worship banned, the Blades disbanded, southern Hammerfell ceded to the Dominion. Hammerfell refused to accept the treaty, fought on alone for five more years, and ultimately forced the Aldmeri out — then broke from the Empire in disgust. The Thalmor used the Concordat to become a feared secret police, actively hunting Talos worshippers across the Empire.
sections.significance: Permanently weakened the Empire and emboldened the Aldmeri Dominion; banned Talos worship and planted the seeds of the Stormcloak Rebellion

sections.title: The Stormcloak Rebellion
sections.content: The Great War's humiliating peace treaty created a powder keg in Skyrim. Ulfric Stormcloak, Jarl of Windhelm, challenged High King Torygg of Skyrim to a duel and killed him using the Voice — an act that split Skyrim in two. The Stormcloak Rebellion pitted pro-independence Nords (who wanted the right to worship Talos and freedom from Imperial law) against the Imperial Legion (who believed only a united Empire could resist the Aldmeri Dominion). Both sides had legitimate arguments, creating a genuine political and philosophical conflict with no obvious correct answer.

factions.name: Stormcloaks
factions.goal: Independence for Skyrim, restoration of Talos worship

factions.name: Imperial Legion
factions.goal: Maintain Imperial unity against the Aldmeri Dominion

sections.title: The Return of Alduin and the Last Dragonborn
sections.content: Alduin, the World Eater — the dragon cast forward through time at the end of the Dragon War — reappeared at the Nordic burial mound of Helgen, disrupting the execution of the future Dragonborn. Alduin began resurrecting ancient dragons from burial mounds across Skyrim, building an army. The Dragonborn was discovered, trained by the Greybeards at High Hrothgar, retrieved an Elder Scroll from the Dwemer ruin of Blackreach, and used it to witness — and learn — the ancient shout Dragonrend. In a climactic confrontation at the Throat of the World, Dragonrend forced Alduin to land, and after a fierce battle Alduin escaped to Sovngarde. The Dragonborn captured Alduin's lieutenant Odahviing and traveled to Skuldafn. In Sovngarde, allied with the ancient Nord heroes Hakon One-Eye, Gormlaith Golden-Hilt, and Felldir the Old, the Dragonborn dispelled Alduin's mist and slew him — not banishing him through time, but truly and permanently ending the World Eater. Many dragons, including Paarthurnax, chose to live in peace afterward.
sections.significance: Permanent defeat of Alduin the World Eater; validation of the Dragonborn as the greatest hero of the age

sections.title: The Dawnguard and the Tyranny of the Sun
sections.content: The ancient Vampire Lord Harkon of the Volkihar clan sought to fulfill a prophecy called the Tyranny of the Sun — using an Elder Scroll and his daughter Serana (sealed away for centuries with the scroll) to blot out the sun permanently and make the entire world a paradise for vampires. The re-formed order of vampire hunters, the Dawnguard, and the Dragonborn foiled this plan. Harkon was slain, the Elder Scroll secured.
sections.game: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim — Dawnguard DLC

sections.title: Miraak and the First Dragonborn
sections.content: The ancient Dragon Priest Miraak — the first Dragonborn, who had turned against the dragons in ages past and been sealed in the realm of Hermaeus Mora — sought to return to Nirn and dominate all dragons and Dragonborn. On the island of Solstheim, Miraak had been secretly exerting control over the islanders. The Last Dragonborn traveled to Hermaeus Mora's realm of Apocrypha and ultimately slew Miraak there, absorbing his soul and becoming definitively the last and greatest Dragonborn.
sections.game: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim — Dragonborn DLC
